title: Congress Escalates the Chip War While China's Domestic Industry Hits Record Revenue
subhead: The MATCH Act would force allies to cut off DUV lithography or face US enforcement, even as Chinese semiconductor firms report record growth and DeepSeek optimizes AI models for Huawei chips instead of Nvidia's ecosystem.

sections.heading: MATCH Act Escalates Semiconductor Controls to Allied Equipment Suppliers
sections.body:
The House Foreign Affairs Committee advanced the MATCH Act on April 22, the largest semiconductor export control markup in congressional history, requiring the Netherlands and Japan to align DUV lithography restrictions with US rules within 150 days or face unilateral American enforcement. The bill would expand the Foreign Direct Product Rule to give Washington jurisdiction over any equipment containing American technology, regardless of manufacturing location. If enacted, ASML would lose its remaining China DUV sales and servicing contracts for machines already installed in every advanced and near-advanced Chinese fab.

The bill names SMIC, Huawei, Hua Hong, CXMT, and YMTC as "covered facilities" and bans allied firms from providing engineering services to maintain machines already operating in Chinese fabs. The Semiconductor Industry Association opposes companion legislation requiring location-verification mechanisms on exported chips, warning of "untested and potentially infeasible" on-chip mechanisms that could undermine global trust in American semiconductors. China's Ministry of Commerce responded that the legislation would "severely disrupt the international economic and trade order."

The structural stakes extend beyond US-China bilateral relations. Japan's Tokyo Electron, Nikon, Canon, and Screen Holdings would face the same deadline. South Korea's SK Hynix and Samsung operate Chinese fabs dependent on servicing from companies the MATCH Act would restrict. ASML projects China's share of revenue dropping from 33% in 2025 to 20% in 2026 without the bill; passage would accelerate that decline dramatically.
sections.whyItMatters: Congress is moving to weaponize American technology embedded in allied equipment, forcing Netherlands and Japan to choose between their China market access and their US supply chains. China has already built regulatory architecture for retaliation through Order No. 834 and mandated 50% domestic equipment procurement.

sections.heading: Chinese Chip Firms Report Record Revenue as US Export Controls Accelerate Self-Sufficiency
sections.body:
SMIC, China's largest chipmaker, reported record revenue of $9.3 billion for 2025, a 16% year-over-year increase, with analysts projecting $11 billion for 2026. Hua Hong posted record fourth-quarter revenue of $659.9 million. Moore Threads, a GPU startup targeting Nvidia's China market share, guided 231-247% revenue growth for 2025. Memory maker CXMT saw 130% revenue growth to $8 billion. The driver: US export controls have "added rocket fuel" to domestic demand, according to Albright Stonebridge Group's Paul Triolo.

The irony is structural. Export restrictions designed to cripple China's semiconductor industry have instead accelerated state-backed procurement mandates and poured capital into domestic alternatives. Beijing now requires Chinese chipmakers to source 50% of equipment from local suppliers, threatening $18 billion in annual US equipment sales. Memory fabs have become "incubators for advanced process technology in ways inconceivable before the October 2022 US export controls."

The constraints remain real. SMIC still cannot access ASML's EUV lithography, limiting it to 7nm production and pilot runs at 5nm. CXMT's HBM2 and HBM2e technologies trail Samsung and SK Hynix by nearly a decade. But the trajectory is toward capacity, not capability — Chinese firms are filling the "compute gap" with volume, not cutting-edge performance.
sections.whyItMatters: US export controls have paradoxically created a protected market for Chinese semiconductor firms, generating record revenues while insulating them from foreign competition. The long-term risk isn't that China will achieve parity on cutting-edge nodes, but that it will build sufficient capacity at near-cutting-edge nodes to power AI inference and military applications.

sections.heading: Starlink Outage Exposes Pentagon's Single Point of Failure in Autonomous Systems
sections.body:
A global Starlink outage last August left two dozen unmanned US Navy vessels bobbing off the California coast, disrupting communications and halting drone tests intended to bolster US military options in a conflict with China, according to internal Navy documents reviewed by Reuters. The incident is one of several Navy test disruptions linked to SpaceX's Starlink that left operators unable to connect with autonomous boats. A separate April 2025 Navy safety report noted that "Starlink reliance exposed limitations under multiple-vehicle load."

The outages highlight a contradiction at the core of Pentagon procurement. SpaceX has secured near-monopoly status for US space launches, provides satellite communications through Starlink and its national security-focused Starshield constellation, and supplies military AI systems. The company is preparing for a $2 trillion public offering this summer — expected to be the largest ever — premised partly on being "indispensable to the US government." But that indispensability creates vulnerability when a single commercial provider's outage degrades military readiness.

Democratic lawmakers have warned about reliance on a company led by the world's richest man. Reuters reported that Musk unexpectedly switched off Starlink access to Ukrainian troops during a 2023 counteroffensive. In Taiwan, SpaceX faced criticism for withholding satellite communications to US service members there, "possibly in breach of SpaceX's contractual obligations." The Pentagon did not respond to questions about the drone test or SpaceX's Navy work.
sections.whyItMatters: SpaceX's dual role as commercial provider and national security partner creates leverage for Musk over US military operations. When a single company controls satellite communications, space launch, and military AI, any outage — technical or political — can cascade into operational paralysis.

sections.heading: AI-Fueled Cyber Proxy Wars Escalate From Website Defacements to Infrastructure Targeting
sections.body:
State-sponsored hackers are using AI agents to automate up to 90% of their attacks, with a clear trajectory shift from website defacements to critical infrastructure targeting, according to a KELA Group report. The main hacktivist groups operate in focal geopolitical conflicts: Ukraine-Russia, Israel-Iran, US-China, and the Korean peninsula. Pro-Iranian hacking group Ababil of Minab claimed responsibility for a March hack on the Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority, publishing claims on Telegram of accessing internal systems. LA Metro shut down portions of its network after finding unauthorized activity.

The April 7 joint CISA advisory warned that Iranian-linked threat groups have exploited programmable logic controllers in water systems, energy infrastructure, and government facilities to cause "disruptive effects within the United States." The report notes that "future hacktivist attacks may target power grids, water treatment facilities, and manufacturing sites" and that "disaster recovery units must prepare for 'total loss' scenarios" where critical information is stolen to cause physical-world damage.

Classic hacking has been replaced by "vibe hacking" — using AI models to craft attacks with deep voice fakes and AI-written phishing emails that are "practically indistinguishable from official communications." Hackers are also using "upstream tactics," targeting SaaS providers rather than specific companies, and extracting sensitive data through prompt manipulation of AI systems that have access to confidential information.
sections.whyItMatters: The barrier to infrastructure-scale cyberattacks is collapsing as AI automation reduces the skill and resource requirements for state-sponsored groups. The plausible deniability of hacktivist proxies creates escalation risks when attacks cause physical-world damage.

sections.heading: EU AI Act Reform Talks Stall as August Compliance Deadline Approaches
sections.body:
Trilogue negotiations between the European Parliament and Council broke down after 12 hours on April 28, leaving high-risk AI systems under Annex III facing the original August 2, 2026 compliance deadline instead of the proposed December 2027 extension. The sticking point: the Parliament's push to shift sectoral legislation from Annex I Section A to B, which the Council resisted. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz backed deregulation of industrial AI, citing products already covered by sectoral regulations. Digital Europe warned of "31 billion euros in unnecessary compliance costs."

The breakdown creates regulatory chaos for European companies that invested in compliance preparation based on expected delays. "Big Tech is probably popping champagne," Dutch MEP Kim van Sparrentak told Reuters, "while European companies that care about safety and did their homework now face regulatory chaos." The Green Party criticized an "alliance between the European People's Party with the far right" in delaying negotiations.

The August deadline covers high-risk AI systems in areas including biometric identification, critical infrastructure, employment, and law enforcement. Companies that prepared for extended timelines now face accelerated compliance requirements or risk penalties. The delay benefits US AI firms with resources to navigate fragmented European regulation, while European startups face disproportionate compliance burdens.
sections.whyItMatters: Regulatory uncertainty in the EU's AI governance creates competitive advantages for well-resourced American and Chinese AI firms, while European startups bear the heaviest compliance costs. The fragmentation risk is that Europe becomes a regulatory fortress that domestic companies can't afford to defend.

sections.heading: White House AI Framework Seeks State Preemption While China's DeepSeek Optimizes for Huawei
sections.body:
The White House released an AI policy framework calling on Congress to preempt state AI laws to ensure "a minimally burdensome national standard consistent with these recommendations, not 50 discordant ones." The framework prioritizes streamlining federal permitting for AI infrastructure, particularly behind-the-meter power generation for data centers, and limiting tech firms' liability for AI-related risks while mandating parental controls and restricting training data. The move follows Trump's December executive order instructing officials to craft a national AI policy.

Simultaneously, DeepSeek — the Chinese AI startup whose low-cost model stunned markets in January 2025 — launched a preview of a new model adapted for Huawei chip technology in late April. DeepSeek has withheld its upcoming flagship model from US chipmakers including Nvidia, breaking from standard industry practice of optimizing for CUDA. The model is optimized for Huawei's Ascend chips, a shift Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang called "a horrible outcome" for the United States because it breaks software dependency on CUDA's ecosystem lock-in.

The parallel trajectories matter. While Washington debates state-level regulatory preemption and liability limits, Chinese AI labs are decoupling from American hardware dependencies. Morgan Stanley forecasts Chinese companies will supply 86% of their country's $67 billion AI chip market by 2030 — one of the fastest semiconductor supply shifts in history.
sections.whyItMatters: The US regulatory debate centers on domestic jurisdictional boundaries while China's AI ecosystem is building independence from American compute infrastructure. The MATCH Act's equipment controls may be too late to prevent software ecosystem decoupling.

bottomLine: The single most important tech-power shift of the day is Congressional escalation of semiconductor export controls through the MATCH Act, which would force allies to cut off DUV lithography to China or face US enforcement — a move that weaponizes American technology embedded in Dutch and Japanese equipment. China's countermeasures are already in place (Order No. 834, rare earth restrictions, 50% domestic equipment mandates), but the structural question is whether export controls accelerate Chinese self-sufficiency or contain it. The evidence suggests both: record revenue for Chinese chipmakers demonstrates protected-market growth, while ASML's EUV restrictions keep cutting-edge nodes out of reach. The longer-term trajectory is that DeepSeek optimizing for Huawei chips rather than Nvidia's CUDA — software ecosystem decoupling — may prove more consequential than hardware restrictions.
sources: Chatham House, Reuters, CNBC, Military Times, Defense One, Jerusalem Post, IAPP, SiliconAngle, Next Web, Forbes, Yahoo Finance, MSN, Politico, Euractiv, Bloomberg, KELA Group, CISA
